explicit_text: I work as a physician assistant at Duke Neuromuscular Outpatient Clinics, and during the COVID-19 pandemic, my roles at Duke expanded significantly. Besides my usual outpatient duties seeing patients with neuromuscular conditions, I also worked on the clinical side of COVID-19 vaccine trials at Duke from September to December 2020. Duke, as a large academic medical center, faced challenges like PPE shortages but managed to support its healthcare workers well throughout the pandemic.
implicit_text: I work as a physician assistant at a prominent neuromuscular outpatient clinic affiliated with a major academic medical center, and during the COVID-19 pandemic, my roles there expanded significantly. Besides my usual outpatient duties seeing patients with neuromuscular conditions, I also worked on the clinical side of COVID-19 vaccine trials at this institution from September to December 2020. This large academic medical center faced challenges like PPE shortages but managed to support its healthcare workers well throughout the pandemic.

explicit_text: The COVID-19 pandemic changed everything about my work and personal life starting in early 2020. I had to take extensive precautions like double masking, wearing face shields, and showering immediately after work to protect my family, especially since I was pregnant in 2020 and had young children. I also worked extra hours on vaccine trials to help support my family when my husband was furloughed in September 2020. The early days were full of fear, uncertainty, and conflicting guidelines, which made it very stressful for healthcare workers like me.

entity: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
explicit_text: During the pandemic, the CDC's guidelines on PPE evolved as we learned more about the virus, but early on, their recommendations were limited by supply shortages. Initially, they advised only surgical masks, then later N95s and double masking, but many of us at Duke took extra precautions beyond CDC guidelines to protect ourselves and our families. The CDC had to balance public health recommendations with what was realistically available to healthcare workers nationwide, which sometimes caused confusion and fear among us.
implicit_text: During the pandemic, the national public health agency's guidelines on PPE evolved as we learned more about the virus, but early on, their recommendations were limited by supply shortages. Initially, they advised only surgical masks, then later N95s and double masking, but many of us at the academic medical center took extra precautions beyond those federal guidelines to protect ourselves and our families. The agency had to balance public health recommendations with what was realistically available to healthcare workers nationwide, which sometimes caused confusion and fear among us.

explicit_text: My sister is an ICU nurse at a large academic medical center in Boston, and she was on a COVID unit during the early weeks of the pandemic. Boston was heavily hit, and in those first weeks, they lacked sufficient N95 masks, so she and her colleagues often had to care for COVID-19 patients wearing only surgical masks until testing confirmed positive cases. This situation was very stressful and highlighted the challenges hospitals faced in the Northeast during the initial surge in 2020.
